coredata.dc:title: Light controllable smart Poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 inhibitor delivery by sustainable green materials for regulation of cytotoxicity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology

coredata.dc:creator: Nazarbek, Guldan
Omarova, Zhuldyz
Shaimoldina, Ainur
Bauyrzhanova, Zhansaya
Kondratyeva, Milana
Zeitkaziyeva, Didar
Onggarbek, Aruzhan
Orassay, Aliya
Fan, Haiyan
Toktarov, Nurlan
Amin, Amr
Xie, Yingqiu
coredata.dc:description: Poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 (PARP) inhibitors have been applied in BRCA-mutated cancers including ovarian, breast and prostate cancers. However, drug resistance may develop by multiple mechanisms. Nanotechnology has advanced drug delivery and reduced drug resistance, but some nano carriers have toxicity, such as carbon dots. Here we report a light controllable drug delivery to cancer cells by chlorophyll forming a self-embedded complex of carbon nanoparticles including PARP inhibitor olaparib, and deer antler extract (4-mix). The light and dark pretreatment were performed with regular light for 6 h and wrapping by foil as dark control respectively. We found light disrupted the compact assembly structure and enhanced the phosphatase nanozyme activity of 4-mix. For cell studies, normal lung cell MRC-5 as control, lung cancer cell A549 was used for comparative analysis. Consistent with the changes in structure, though 6 h regular light treatment reduced the cytotoxicity in lung cancer A549 cells, overall dark enhanced the efficiency of 4-mix compared with olaparib alone by more than three-fold (20% cell viability vs 65% viability). The above effect is counteracting in the normal lung cell line of MRC-5 suggesting the cancer associated controllable cytotoxicity effect. Thus, light controlled drug delivery may reduce side effects of cytotoxicity of carbon dots materials. Most importantly, we provided insights of light controlled drug response for reducing side effects of nano carriers for potent tumor spatial delivery by green sustainable materials from natural products to target PARP, an important drug target in cancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Olaparib
Smart delivery
Nanozyme
PARP inhibitor
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nanoparticles and four-component complexes
Atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning and transmission electron microscopy (SEM, TEM)
Fluorescence absorbance, fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and zeta potential analysis of the formulations
Morphological properties of modulated olaparib 4-mix particles
Zeta potential analysis of modulated olaparib 4-mix particles
Fluorescence analysis of modulated olaparib 4-mix particles
Light controls the enzymatic activity of modulated olaparib 4-mix particles
Light controls efficacy of the modulated olaparib 4-mix particles in cancer cells
Potential static charges contribute to the assembly of the modulated olaparib 4-mix
Ethics approval and consent to participate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by Nazarbayev University FDCRGP Grant with Funder Project Reference No. 110119FD4531, 11022021FD2920, 040225FD4744, and AUA- UAEU grant 12R118.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of morphology of particles assembly with olaparib, beet CD, chlorophyll, and antler extracts mixtures (4-mix). A) SEM; B) Size distribution based on images of SEM; C) TEM; D) Effect of light exposure (for 7 h) on particles; E) Diagram indicates the assembly of the 4-mix and light effect.

Characterization of morphology of particles of 4-mix by AFM imaging. A) AFM of 4 mix, Height (Dec) 3.5 μm and 5 μm AFM images and distribution graph of 4mix; B) AFM; Height (Dec) 5 μm AFM images and distribution graph of olaparib alone.
Characterization of size of particles of 4-mix by zeta potential analysis and light exposure effect. A) Volume measurement; B) electrical potential.
Characterization of 4-mix by A) FT-IR; B) Fluorescence spectrum.

Nanozyme activities of olaparib, beet CD, chlorophyll, and antler extracts mixture assembly (4-mix) by A) substrate NBT/BCIP at pH 7; B) ALP as control, and 4- mix nanozyme of phosphatase activity by light and no light with NBT/BCIP at pH 7; C) ALP and 4-mix (under light) at different pH with NBT/BCIP; D) 4-mix (under light and no light) with different substrate concentrations of NBT/BCIP at pH 7. For statistically analysis results, ∗, p < 0.05; ∗∗, p < 0.01.

Comparison of cell viability under light and dark in A549 cells upon treatment with 4-mix and olaparib alone control. A,B) Cell viability under light or no light in cells indicated as A549 and MRC-5 cells; C) Confocal microscope analysis of 4-mix cellular distribution by auto-fluorescence and fluorescence staining with DAPI at AF488 and AF594. Magnification: 60×. D) Gel electrophoresis of light and dark exposed 4-mix particles. Based on the autofluorescence of CD particles and chlorophyll extract, red arrow indicates the chlorophyll extract and blue arrows indicate CD associated mixtures and diffusion of chlorophyll from 4-mix compact complex. For statistically analysis results, ∗, p < 0.05; ∗∗, p < 0.01. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Light controllable smart Poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 inhibitor delivery by sustainable green materials for regulation of cytotoxicity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 (PARP) inhibitors have been applied in BRCA-mutated cancers including ovarian, breast and prostate cancers. However, drug resistance may develop by multiple mechanisms. Nanotechnology has advanced drug delivery and reduced drug resistance, but some nano carriers have toxicity, such as carbon dots. Here we report a light controllable drug delivery to cancer cells by chlorophyll forming a self-embedded complex of carbon nanoparticles including PARP inhibitor olaparib, and deer antler extract (4-mix). The light and dark pretreatment were performed with regular light for 6 h and wrapping by foil as dark control respectively. We found light disrupted the compact assembly structure and enhanced the phosphatase nanozyme activity of 4-mix. For cell studies, normal lung cell MRC-5 as control, lung cancer cell A549 was used for comparative analysis. Consistent with the changes in structure, though 6 h regular light treatment reduced the cytotoxicity in lung cancer A549 cells, overall dark enhanced the efficiency of 4-mix compared with olaparib alone by more than three-fold (20% cell viability vs 65% viability). The above effect is counteracting in the normal lung cell line of MRC-5 suggesting the cancer associated controllable cytotoxicity effect. Thus, light controlled drug delivery may reduce side effects of cytotoxicity of carbon dots materials. Most importantly, we provided insights of light controlled drug response for reducing side effects of nano carriers for potent tumor spatial delivery by green sustainable materials from natural products to target PARP, an important drug target in cancer therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer presents the most serious threat to public health worldwide. Its complexity and the absence of effective drugs complicate the treatment. Olaparib is an inhibitor targeting poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase-1 (PARP-1, PARP) enzyme by DNA repairing disruption, subsequently leading to tumor cell death [1]. However, cancer cells can acquire resistance to olaparib by various mechanisms upon treatment and cancer adaption [1]. One of the resistance mechanisms is a reverse or a secondary mutation of BRCA1/2 genes. BRCA1 and BRCA2 locate on chromosome 17q12-21 and 13q12-13 respectively. The two genes predispose about 80% of families related cases of women's breast cancer in which BRCA1 germline mutations also associate with ovarian cancer while BRCA2 pancreatic cancer, prostate cancer and male breast cancer [2]. In addition, the primary mutation of BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes blocks their ability to repair damaged DNA and increases the risks of cancer development [1,3]. However, a second mutation corrects the original conversion and restores the genes' function, thus inducing the non-responsiveness of cancer cells towards DNA damaging targeted chemotherapies [2]. Moreover, the reactivation of BRCA1/2 proteins contributes to the homologous recombination (HR) pathway, decreasing the level of dsDNA damage [3].

Considering that olaparib has a great potential to be applied in various cancers in clinical treatment such as ovarian, fallopian, primary peritoneal carcinomas, pancreatic, prostate, and breast cancers, the anti-cancer utilization of PARP inhibitors (PARPi) requires additional modulation to evade the drug resistance. Proteomics technologies with mass spectrometry or protein array have advanced the dissection of the underlying molecular mechanisms of several types of cancer, including analysis of adaptive responses to therapy, to develop precision approaches for overcoming drug resistance and improving treatment outcomes [3]. As an alternatively approach, it has been shown that using photosensitizer methylene blue and PLGA with PARPi veliparib, forming complex of 90 nm sized nanoparticles increased tumor inhibition efficacy through photodynamics [4]. DNA replication can be disrupted by the PARP-DNA binding in which PARP trapping is essential for the cytotoxicity of PARP inhibitors suggesting the potent higher efficiency of evaluated olaparib, niraparib, and rucaparib inhibitors than that of veliparib through PARP trapping [5]. Here, simulating the PARP trapping mechanisms, we aimed to modulate PARPi using nanoparticles from different resources with controllable activity.

Deer antler extract, as a traditional medicine, plays roles in the healthy state of bones, anti-bacteria, anti-virus, anti-cancer, anti-oxidative stress and inflammation, and promoting physical endurance of athletes and anti-fatigue [6]. Kuo et al. (2018) reported the anti-inflammatory properties of antler extracts involving the regulation of interleukins and cytokines signaling [7]. For example, a single-chain peptide within the antler plate was shown to inhibit the growth of MA782 rat breast cancer cells by targeting telomerase function [8]. Chlorophyll is the non-toxic green pigment naturally found in plants. According to a recent study, considering the ability of hydrophobic chlorophyll molecules to regulate the conversion of photosensitizers into monomers and enhancing their therapeutic advantages, the system composed of chlorophyll extract, Copaifera reticulata Ducke plant, and emulgel showed high potential to act as a powerful agent against cutaneous leishmania and enhance the effect of the photodynamic therapy, which is also used in cancer treatment [9]. Besides, chlorophyll was studied in terms of photothermal therapy using polymer micelles [9]. These nanocomposites allowed effective diagnosis of tumour due to the rapid targeting of affected cells and emitted fluorescence during in vivo and in vitro imaging [10]. Therefore, the photothermal and photodynamic properties of chlorophyll might provide crucial modulations in developing therapeutic complexes.

Carbon dots (CD) also have various properties that make them suitable for drug delivery and therapeutic applications. For instance, carbon dots are reported to exhibit antimicrobial activity [11], biocompatibility or toxicity depending on resources of synthesis [12], and fluorescence properties [13]. Carbon nanoparticles may induce different types of cell death [13], including apoptosis, ferroptosis, necrosis, and carboptosis [14]. Besides such beneficial properties, these nanoparticles can be applied for delivery of PARP inhibitors, including olaparib, by improving their solubility and targeted cellular localizations [14]. These above-mentioned components present a high potential for their combined use with PARPi, while their drug delivery properties require a more thorough investigation. In this study, we combined olaparib with beet-derived CD, chlorophyll, deer antler extract, and compared the light-dark effect on cell viability which could have a potential in alternative controllable cancer therapy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nanoparticles and four-component complexes
ce:section.ce:para: First, CD synthesis was performed by hydrothermal method as described previously using 100 mL autoclave reactor with dried beet powder, 24 mL ethylenediamine and 36 mL of ddH2O for reaction at 200 °C for 5 h [14]. The solution product was collected by centrifuge and purified by dialysis for 48 h (1000 Da) followed by vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight [14]. Next, samples of 4-mix including olaparib (Selleck Chemicals, Cologne, Germany) (10 μM stock), beet CD (50 mg/mL stock) [14], chlorophyll (50 mg/mL crude extract [15]), and antler extract (10X company stock, from commercially available gift from Kazakhstan KATON-KARAGAI DEER PARK GROUP OF COMPANIES and Intrade company and the commercially available extract was directly used for analysis) were prepared in 1:1: 1:7 ratio. by stir and stand for 1 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atomic force microscopy (AFM), scanning and transmission electron microscopy (SEM, TEM)
ce:section.ce:para: High resolution AFM analysis was performed using AFM Smart SPM 1000 (AIST-NT, Russia). Samples were put on a coverslip for air dry with PBS washing before performing investigation and imaging by AFM. For TEM, samples in solution were loaded on PELCO grids for investigation. ZEISS Crossbeam 540 electron microscope (Carl Zeiss) was used for SEM analysis by dropping a sample on a carrier and air-drying it. JEOL JEM-1400 Plus electron microscope was applied for TEM analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence absorbance, fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and zeta potential analysis of the formulations
ce:section.ce:para: Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrometer was used to analyze fluorescence absorbance of each sample (olaparib, beet CD, chlorophyll, and antler extracts) and their mixes (4-mix). The emission and excitation wavelengths were 350 nm, while the data was recorded within the 400–600 nm range. OMNIC FT-IR spectrum within the range of 500–3500 wavenumber/cm-1 was applied to measure samples.

Zeta potential analyzer, ZetaView TWIN Laser (PMX-230) system was used to measure the zeta potential values of the formulations both on size, volume and zeta potential at room temperature of 25 °C. For 4-mix at dark condition, the number of average counted particles per frame is 183 and total number of traced particles are 5686; For 4-mix at light condition, the number of average counted particles per frame is 170 and number of traced particles are 1408. Samples were diluted as 1:1000 for dark treated samples, and 1:100 for light treated samples.

Cells were seeded on coverslips in 6-well plates for 24 h then fixed for 15 min at room temperature, as described previously [16]. Next cells were stained with DAPI for 1 min followed by sealing with anti-fade mounting medium. Confocal images were obtained by scanning using Zeiss LSM 780 Confocal Microscope and analyzed by ZEN lite software.

A549 and MRC-5 cell lines (by procurement from Department of Biology, NU from Education from ATCC) were applied for the 4-mix effect on cell survival. Cells were seeded on 24 well plates for 24 h then treated with nanoparticles as indicated in the figures. Next, cells were continued growing for 2–3 days followed by crystal violet staining as indicated previously [16]. Optical density (OD) was measured by collecting 30 % ethanol soluble dye solutions into 96 well plates for reading. Control cells were applied as relative 100 % viability and treated cells were compared to calculate the percentage based on OD value.

Nanoparticles mixture (4-mix) simulated enzyme activity was measured by substrates NBT and BCIP of Thermo Scientific™ 1-Step™ NBT/BCIP Substrate Solution (Thermo Scientific Pierce) with ready-use solution of nitro-blue tetrazolium and 5-bromo-4-chloro-3′-indolyphosphate.

Agarose gel (1 %) was applied for electrophoresis gel analysis of 4-mix compounds with 100 V with 25 min. The light pretreatment were performed with regular light for 6 h and dark via wrapped foil as control respectively.

Student T-test was applied for statistical analysis using Microsoft excel. p < 0.05 or p < 0.01 was believed as statistically significant and statistically highly significant differences respectively. Two tails distribution and two-sample equal variance were used as conditions for T-test. For statistically analysis results indicated in figures, ∗ means p < 0.05; ∗∗ means p < 0.01.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological properties of modulated olaparib 4-mix particles
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of nanoparticles was analyzed by using SEM, TEM and AFM imaging. The synthesized beet CD, olaparib, chlorophyll, and antler extract, 4-mixed nanoparticles have a round shape, the rough surface texture and are assembled with a homogeneous size ranging from 150 to 550 nm investigated by SEM (Fig. 1 A and B). TEM imaging of the assembled 4-mixed nanoparticles, showed that small particles surrounded the larger particles forming a coat with more details for inner particles assembled complex formation (Fig. 1C). Interestingly, light exposed 4-mix showed a disruption of round impact shape, with loosen structures in the center and size ranging from 200 to 2000 nm (majority as 800–950 nm), which might be due to chlorophyll response generated disassembly of the 4-mix (Fig. 1D and E).

Similarly to SEM images, the same morphology characteristics of non-light exposed 4-mix can be observed from AFM imaging that nanoparticles were being well-dispersed throughout space (Fig. 2 A) while olaparib alone showed nanoscale particles surface was less than 4 mixed size as about predominately 40–60 nm (Fig. 2B). AFM measurement usually has limitations and obtaining the high-resolution images is technically challenging. Due to the time limitation and reproducing ability, different height images may reflect and support the similar view as we provided the different height images too. Our data suggest the potent associations of the 4 types of particles by assembly and light controllable of the impact of the assembly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential analysis of modulated olaparib 4-mix particles
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the particles' characterization by a different approach, we used zeta potential analyzer to measure particles' 3D volume and electrical potential of the slipping plane. We first found that light exposure increased the particle's volume peak from about 0.5 nm3 in dark condition to 1.0 nm3 upon light exposure for particle size range of peak from 250 nm to 350 nm (Fig. 3 A). This is consistent with the above-investigated larger size of the particle upon light exposure, suggesting the light control of the 4-mix assembly. In addition, light increased the peak of frequency of the positive charged particles, and reduced the 2nd peak of frequency of dark 4-mix with SL1 about −2.834 mV (Fig. 3B), suggesting the light mediated surface changes in charge, reflecting the 4-mix assembly loose by releasing more negative charged particles.

To reveal changes in the chemical characteristics upon assembly of modulated olaparib as 4-mix particles, spectroscopy indicating FT-IR absorption and frequency was employed. Fig. 4 A indicates the spectrum of six experimental samples within the 500-3500 cm-1 wavenumber range. The similar peaks are the C-H stretching between 2800 and 3000 cm-1 and C=O stretching at approximately 1650 cm-1 (Fig. 4A). The peak around 1550 cm-1 represents N-H bending (Fig. 4A). Although the absorption and frequency patterns were nearly identical, 4-mix including chlorophyll, olaparib, antler extract, and beet CDs showed much higher intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence analysis of modulated olaparib 4-mix particles
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the quantum features of the 4-mix compared to the quantum dots, beet CD alone, fluorescence spectrum was analyzed. Low fluorescence intensities were generated by the water control, antler extract, and chlorophyll, while the sample of 4-mix and beet CD generated a higher fluorescence intensity. In the dark, the fluorescence intensity of the 4 mixes was approximately 230 a.u., and about 450 nm (Fig. 4B). A similar trend was observed to that of the beet CD, but the strongest fluorescence is identified at 470 nm. On the other hand, with the light stimulation, the fluorescence intensity of the 4 mixes showed a bell-shaped spectrum within the 400–600 nm range with the intensity enhanced more than twice (Fig. 4B), which is similar to CD alone. Our data suggest light treatment may result in the 4-mix complex loosened by decreasing binding affinity, related to the chlorophyll conformation changes by light. Thus, dark may sustain the original complex assembly and potent function in drug delivery, which is indicated in changes in the morphology with swelling and loose trend (Fig. 1E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light controls the enzymatic activity of modulated olaparib 4-mix particles
ce:section.ce:para: Given that CD has the potent phosphatase-like nanozyme activity, we then tested whether the 4-mix assembly could affect the phosphatase activity and whether the enzyme activity could be light controllable. Using NBT/BCIP as phosphatase substrate, we found that the phosphatase activity of the 4-mix was similar to that of the CD alone (p = 0.120) while antler extract and chlorophyll did not show any significant phosphatase-like activity (Fig. 5 A). Compared with the activity in the dark condition, light stimulated the phosphatase-like activity of the 4-mix much more significantly than that of dark (p < 0.05, Fig. 5B). Next we tested the pH effect on the enzymatic activity. A relatively lower phosphatase-like activity of the 4-mix was detected at pH 4.7, 7, and 9.92, while the 4-mix showed a strong enzyme activity at pH 13 (p < 0.05, Fig. 5C). Enzymatic kinetics analysis showed a relative lower slope of the 4-mix with increasing concentration of NBT/BCIP substrate suggesting as a nanozyme, the 4-mix simulates a maximum activity and efficient reaction with the substrate similar to a protein-based enzyme (Fig. 5D). Consistent with the above investigation, though the kinetics are similar between light and without light, the Vmax of light exposure are higher than that of without light. In summary, our data suggest light may disrupt the sustained original complex assembly, thereby releasing the CD, and olaparib, and exposing more nanozyme surface for reaction efficiency with enhanced enzyme activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light controls efficacy of the modulated olaparib 4-mix particles in cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the biological function of light controllable modulated olaparib 4-mix particles, we applied lung cancer A549, and normal MRC5, cell lines. We selected lung cancer cell line because the lung cancer is the largest cancer incidence in the world accounting for the highest mortality rates among both men and women. MRC-5 cell line (https://www.atcc.org/products/ccl-171) was originally developed from the normal lung tissue of a male embryo which can be used as a normal cell control for the A549 cell line, which is a human lung carcinoma that was derived from a male patient. As A549 cell line has also been widely used in studies on human lung by toxic substances and nanoparticles. (https://www.atcc.org/products/ccl-185), we decided to use the two cell lines for further studies.

In comparison with olaparib alone, the 4-mix showed enhanced efficacy in suppressing the cell viability of A549 by more than three-fold (20 % cell viability vs 65 % viability), but not MRC-5 cells (p < 0.05, Fig. 6 A and B). This observation was made in the absence of light and these results were reversed under the light (Fig. 6A and B). In addition to these results, the treatment of A549 cells with 4 mixed compounds exhibited a prominent decrease in cell viability compared to CD alone, suggesting the potent synergistic efficacy of the 4-mix with CD induced cell signaling pathways as we have reported that CD may target PARP pathways and inducing DNA damage [14] (Supplementary Fig. S1).

Furthermore, the treatment of HeLa cells with 4 mixed compounds exhibited a prominent decrease in cell viability compared to olaparib alone (Supplementary Fig. S1). Our data suggest the potent 4-mix-based drug encapsulation would enhance the PARP inhibitor efficacy.

Moreover, we found the 4-mix can enter the nucleus even all over the cells (Fig. 6C). Importantly, merged images showed the potent colocalization of carbon dots (AF 488) and chlorophyll (red fluorescence) as yellow colors suggesting the assembly complex formation, at least, in part can be formed in cells too (Fig. 6C, Supplementary Fig. S2). This indicates the potent application in bioimaging or nucleus targeting of DNA repair by the 4-mix for cancer during the treatment in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential static charges contribute to the assembly of the modulated olaparib 4-mix
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the potential mechanism of the 4-mix complex assembly, we used electrophoresis gel analysis of 4-mix to measure the potent charge. We found the light induced a diffusion of the band suggesting the charges associated compact loose (Fig. 6D). Further chlorophyll extract and CD complex investigation by electrophoresis gel analysis showed the chlorophyll extract can run toward the positive charged node while CD and 4 mix still remain positive charged (Fig. 6D; Supplementary Fig. S3). This indicates the negative charged chlorophyll extract may help assembly and impact complex formation with positive charged CD by statics attraction and the light which interacts with chlorophyll extract may disrupt the 4-mix complex. Consistent with this hypothesis, we previously reported that CD has positive charge overall by gel electrophoresis analysis [14]. Thus, our data suggest that potential static charges contribute to the assembly of the 4-mix, while light may disrupt and complex due to light sensitive materials of chlorophyll extract thereby interfering the PARPi efficacy and cytotoxicity.

In summary of above results with morphology, surface effect, charge effect, nanozyme activity, and cell vitality, our data suggest that light may control the 4-mix efficacy of PARPi possibly by forming self-assembly complex and light controllable for reducing cytotoxicity of nano carrier, CD (Fig. 1E).

Here we reported a smart drug delivery system by the 4-mix and the light controllable regulation of the cancer cell viability upon treatment. In previous studies, olaparib benefited patients with platinum-sensitive relapsed ovarian cancer by reducing fatal outcomes and preventing further cancer progression [17], and anticancer in breast cancer [18], and lung cancer [19,20]. However, the effectiveness of olaparib as an anticancer drug is limited due to the resistance acquired through epigenetic modifications of related genes, homologous recombination repair restoration (HRR), and several other mechanisms such as nonhomologous end joining (NHEJ) which are essential for repairing double-strand DNA breaks (DSB) [21]. HRR pathways are functional in the S/G2 phase using sister chromatid as template, whereas NHEJ repairs DSB throughout all cell cycle phases except the M phase [21]. Involved proteins include Ku70/80, DNA-PKcs, Artemis, DNA pol λ/μ, DNA ligase IV-XRCC4, and XLF, PAXX, MRI/CYREN, TARDBP of TDP-43, IFFO1, ERCC6L2, and RNase H2. MRI/CYREN is a switch as it stimulates NHEJ in the G1 phase but inhibits the repair in the S and G2 phases [21,22]. To address problems with PARPi-associated cancer therapies, this study provided an alternative method to control olaparib-based 4-mix mediated cell viability which has great application potential in clinical applications. How the 4-mix could overcome resistance by interactions with the above proteins would be warrantee for clarify the details in mechanisms in future research.

Carbon dots are luminescent nanomaterials that exhibit remarkable emission properties [14]. Although beet CD displayed moderate fluorescence intensity, when mixed with olaparib compound, antler, and chlorophyll, there was a significant increase in fluorescence intensity under light conditions. Moreover, the emission peak was red-shifted compared to the mix sample without light.

The results indicate that despite the low phosphatase activity of antler and chlorophyll alone, olaparib in combination with beet CD, antler, and chlorophyll exhibited enhanced phosphatase activity. Notably, the enzymatic activity of the mixed sample is controllable and negatively correlated to cell viability suggesting the potent distinct signaling and function in targeting kinase signing in light and dark systems. However, in normal conditions, it is positively correlated with cell viability. It indicates the phosphatase activity of the nanozyme may induce kinase inhibition in normal conditions, which exhibit a low level of kinase expression or activity thereby showing the high efficacy. In cancer cells, the light may disrupt the assembly as shown in the morphology analysis by SEM, which disassociates the complex and thereby decreasing the efficacy of the 4-mix mediated co-targeting of DNA repair protein, PARP and its associated phosphorylation complex. Thus, in cancer patient, we may control the onsite organ targeting for precision therapy especially when multiple tissues developing cancer by light control to specially target special cancer cells. We may further explore the new technology of single cell targeting by light in future.

Cell viability assays showed enhanced cell apoptosis of MRC-5 cells under light conditions after treatment with the mixed compound, compared to olaparib treatment alone. Conversely, in the dark condition, olaparib showed better efficacy in targeting MRC-5 cells. Another possible mechanism is chlorophyll-mediated cytotoxicity. Previous studies have proposed chlorophyll-mediated therapies for cancer viability suppression [23]. For instance, chlorophyll incorporated onto the surface of vanadium carbide nanostructures provided enhanced photothermal and photodynamic therapy by generating oxygen (O2) in the hypoxic tumour microenvironment and reactive oxygen species (ROS) upon light exposure [24]. These findings align with our results, where chlorophyll within the evaluated four mix samples likely promoted the decrease in MRC-5 cell viability after light exposure.

On the other hand, the cell viability assay of A549 cells demonstrated a better effect of olaparib and lower efficacy of the four mixed compounds to kill cells under light exposure. Without light exposure, A549 cells exhibited better suppression in cell viability after 4-mix compound treatment, compared to olaparib alone. However, according to multiple studies, there is a difference between the photosensitivity variation in cancerous and normal cells [25,26]. Normally, cancer cells are more susceptible to photodynamic therapy due to their better uptake of photosensitizer, increased ROS production, altered cell signaling pathways, and lack of defense mechanisms [23,26]. Moreover, the result of our study indicates that synthesized 4-mix compounds have better cell growth inhibition of normal MRC-5 cells under the light condition, while lower growth suppression of cancerous A549 cells. However, the effectiveness of such nanoparticles in suppressing cancerous cells like A549 cells can be influenced by various factors, and certain conditions could lead to limited or no suppression. Previously, it was reported that phototoxicity decreases in cell viability was observed solely in nanocapsules with a negatively charged zeta potential on the outer layer. In contrast, nanocapsules with a positively charged zeta potential on the outer layer exhibited cell viability rates about 80% for A549 cells and 60% for MRC-5 cells [26]. The synthesis of 4-mix compounds might cause surface functionalization and alter the presence of functional groups on the surface of nanoparticles, contributing to the change in the zeta potential and altering the effect of nanoparticles on cancerous cells. The observation correlated with electrophoresis results, indicating the positive surface net charge.

Finally, the light controlled disassembly and photodynamic PARPi therapy [27,28] has not been widely studied and here we found the 4-mix as we developed nanocomposite may reduce the cytotoxicity of CDs under the light, which may reduce the side effect to enhance the specificity of the 4-mix. We and others have shown that CDs and natural products such as plant medicine and herbs extract, may cause toxicity in cancer cells with phosphatase activity [29–34]. The non-targeting application of the toxicity may produce side effect for chemotherapy or targeted therapy. Thus, applying light controllable smart delivery of olaparib may avoid the nano carrier induced side effect, especially at high dose. Moreover, we can not exclude the tumor microenvironment induced regulation and more future investigations would allow us to clarify the controllable drug delivery with microevneorment interactions [16].

It has been shown that chlorophyll can transfer electrons and may appear negative charged based on the function and structure [35]. Our preliminary investigation of chlorophyll extract by electrophoresis gel analysis showed the chlorophyll extract can run toward the positive charged node while CDs and 4-mix still remain positive charged (Fig. 6D). This indicates the negative charged chlorophyll extract which may help assembly and impact complex by statics attraction. However, the light which interacts with chlorophyll extract may disrupt the 4-mix complex. Consistent with this hypothesis, we previously reported that CD has positive charge [14]. Thus, we may propose that the potential static charges contribute to the assembly of the 4-mix, while light may disrupt the complex due to light sensitive materials of chlorophyll extract thereby interfering the PARPi efficacy and cytotoxicity. However, more further studies by different approaches to investigate total charges and surface charges of the 4-mix and chlorophyll-CD complex, would clarify the potent mechanisms of assembly.

The formation of the beet CD-chlorophyll-antler extracts incorporated with olaparib holds promise as a therapy that targets normal and cancer cells under specific conditions by light control. While the most components of 4-mix showed anticancer function such as deer antler extract protein, SDAPR [36], chemical compound [37,38] (Fig. 1E), CDs [14] and PARP inhibitor, further studies on synergistic effect and antagonistic effect would allow us to understand the complex mediated cell signaling targeting cancer cells. Moreover, environmental green regulation such as sun light and CO2 on anticancer efficiency of drug delivery would be promising under a friendly ecosystem or smart delivery systems [39–46]. Overall, these observations suggest a synergetic, enhanced effect of the mixed compounds in cancer treatment which could serve as a potential alternative treatment strategy under certain conditions. Further exploring the optimal treatment conditions in vivo in animal models, and testing the efficacy of the mixed compound needs a further complicated analysis for improving the effectiveness of olaparib-incorporated mixed nanoparticle compound in cancerous cell treatment.

For clinical applications, protection from light could be realized by dark packaging and in vivo treatment of light insensitive organ sites or tissues such as liver, lung, stomach, colon, prostate. While light may disrupt the efficiency of the 4-mix composite, skin cancer would not be suitable for the treatment by the 4-mix agent. To reduce side effects, the light therapy can be combined for non-cancer organs. Although under the light the 4-mix also showed toxicity to normal cell MRC-5, the light can disrupt the 4-mix by releasing olaparib and prevent DNA damage. For other competent toxicity under light, we can consider dose reducation. Moreover, further investigations in vivo mouse models and clinical trials would be a warranty for our clarification of side effects of the 4-mix to improve it by adjusting doses or ratio of the 4-mix.

Important to the field of the research is that we provided a light-controlled drug response for reducing the side effects of nano carriers via green sustainable materials from natural products and herbs to target PARP in combination with PARP inhibitors in cancer therapy. Integration of Chinese medicine with western medicine by precision targeted therapy is still challenging [47]. Here we applied 3 resource-derived, traditional Chinese Medicine-based, combination targeting of PARP in cancer to overcome potent drug resistance of PARP inhibitors. The drug resistance is a large obstacle in clinical application of PARP inhibitor drugs, due to multiple mechanisms such as activation of additional signaling mTOR, cell inflammation, STAT3, clonal evolution of stem-like, hypoxia pathways, epigenetic re-expression of BRCA1, elevation of drug transporters, loss of 53BP1, homologous recombination repair restoration, high C/EBPβ expression induced restoration of homologous recombination, restoration of replication fork stability, degradation of stalled replication forks, upregulation of High-mobility group box 3 (HMGB3) [48]. Targeting PARP by light regulation would at least be in different tissues to control DNA repair complementary function by BRCA gene family, as light controllable PARPi activity would at least allow PARPi delivery to the needed tissues or cells to avoid the accumulation of adjacent cells to stimulate the resistance signaling. For example, drug transporters' elevation usually needs a time-period of adaptation and elevation, which is not an immediate response. While controllable PARPi delivery and treatment in different cells would allow the spatial regulation of tumor area and prevent the systemic resistance and mutagenesis. Moreover, CDs are also synergistic with PARPi at a moderate dose [14] and disrupt the cytoskeleton [29]. Deer antler extract can function as phosphatase cofactor [49] in potent dephosphorylating of kinase themselves phosphorylation or their substrate/target phosphorylation, which is an essential event in PARPi resistance such as MET-mediated phosphorylation of PARP in drug resistance [50]. Chlorophyll can also exert anticancer function [23]. Thus, our innovation is using 4 components to create a light controllable cytotoxicity to use multiple mechanisms or pathways to target DNA repair, which can promote targeting DNA and combined inhibition of DNA repair. Multiple signaling targeting is one of the strategies to prevent drug resistance, but we applied natural resources-derived components in the 4-mix, which has ecological benefit with unknown drug resistance development reported.

This study provided a light controllable complex of the PARP inhibitor, olaparib, with beet carbon dots, chlorophyll, and antler extract and characterized light stimulated morphological features of less compact, dual fluorescence mediated imaging, phosphatase-like activity, and cytotoxicity. Light may control the suppression of A549 cells by the 4-mix targeting PARP and phosphatase signaling for anticancer applications. Thus, natural product or food derived particles may be applied for drug delivery and controlled efficacy to reduce side effects on different tumor spatial regulation.

Guldan Nazarbek: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhuldyz Omarova: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Ainur Shaimoldina: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhansaya Bauyrzhanova: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Milana Kondratyeva: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Didar Zeitkaziyeva: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Aruzhan Onggarbek: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Aliya Orassay: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Haiyan Fan: Resources. Nurlan Toktarov: Resources. Amr Amin: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Yingqiu Xie: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
Data will be provided upon reasonable request to Yingqiu Xie, and Guldan Nazarbek.
This work was funded by Nazarbayev University FDCRGP Grant with Funder Project Reference No. 110119FD4531, 11022021FD2920, 040225FD4744, and AUA-UAEU grant 12R118.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Vitaliy Milov, Aruzhan Madet, Akbota Sergazina, Sandugash Myrzagali, Lei Shi for the assistance of the experiment or editing of draft or preparing of figures.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanoparticles neutralize carbon dioxide (CO2) in cytotoxicity: potent carbon emission induced resistance to anticancer nanomedicine and antibiotics
Ecotoxicol. Environ. Saf.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shaimoldina A, Sergazina A, Myrzagali S, Nazarbek G, Omarova Z, Mirza O, Fan H, Amin A, Zhou W, Xie Y. Carbon nanoparticles neutralize carbon dioxide (CO2) in cytotoxicity: Potent carbon emission induced resistance to anticancer nanomedicine and antibiotics. Ecotoxicol Environ Saf. 2024 Mar 15;273:116024. doi: 10.1016/j.ecoenv.2024.116024. Epub 2024 Feb 22. PMID: 38394753.
